BLABLA2

title: BiomeXplore project

1.2 Experimental design (brief)

Species: Scophthalmus maximus (turbot)

Tissue: (e.g. skin / intestine / specify when ready)

Library type: Stranded mRNA-seq

Sequencing platform: Illumina

Analysis pipeline: nf-core/rnaseq

Detailed methodological information is provided in the corresponding Methods section.

title: BiomeXplore project
text:
1.3 Structure of the report
The analysis is organised into the following sections:

Introduction
Biological background and motivation of the transcriptomic study.
Methods (nf-core / RNA-seq pipeline)
Description of the computational workflow, software versions and key parameters.
Quality control (MultiQC)
Assessment of sequencing quality, mapping performance and quantification metrics.
Alignment and quantification
Summary of alignment results and expression estimates.
Differential expression analysis
Identification of differentially expressed genes and downstream interpretation.
crumbs: 1  Overview

title: BiomeXplore project
text:
1.4 Reproducibility and data provenance
All analyses were performed on the CESGA HPC infrastructure.
Pipeline outputs were generated using nf-core standards and selectively integrated into this report to ensure clarity, traceability and reproducibility.
crumbs: 1  Overview

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
2.1 Overview
This document describes a reproducible workflow for processing paired-end shotgun metagenomic reads using KneadData for host-read depletion and quality control, followed by Meteor2 for taxonomic, functional, and optional strain-level profiling. The workflow is designed for host-associated microbiome datasets, particularly human gut shotgun metagenomes, and reflects the command-line interfaces and recommendations documented in the official KneadData and Meteor repositories.
The pipeline is structured in two major stages:
The expected final outputs are:
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
Preprocessing and host-read removal with KneadData
Metagenomic profiling with Meteor2



host-depleted, high-quality paired FASTQ files from KneadData
per-sample gene count tables from Meteor2 mapping
per-sample taxonomic and functional abundance profiles from Meteor2 profiling
merged multi-sample abundance tables for downstream analysis
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
3.1 1. Purpose
KneadData is a quality-control and decontamination tool designed for metagenomic and metatranscriptomic sequencing data, especially microbiome datasets. In host-associated samples, sequencing libraries often contain a substantial fraction of host-derived reads. KneadData performs in silico separation of microbial reads from contaminant reads, typically using:

Trimmomatic for adapter/quality trimming
TRF for optional low-complexity/repetitive sequence filtering
Bowtie2 for contaminant/host read removal against a reference database

In this workflow, KneadData is used to remove human reads before downstream profiling with Meteor2.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
3.2 2. Installation

3.2.1 2.1 Recommended installation with Conda
A Conda installation is recommended because it resolves the main dependencies required by KneadData.
conda create -n kneaddata -c bioconda kneaddata -y
conda activate kneaddata
This installation typically provides the core software required to run the workflow, including:

KneadData
Bowtie2
Trimmomatic
Java runtime support

Alternative installation methods exist, including pip and Docker, but Conda is the preferred option for a reproducible local CLI workflow.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 3.3 3. Download the human host reference database
text:
3.3 3. Download the human host reference database
KneadData provides helper commands to download reference databases compatible with Bowtie2.
kneaddata_database --download human_genome bowtie2 $DIR
Where:

$DIR is the directory where the reference database will be stored


3.3.1 Notes

For human host depletion, the recommended database is the human hg39 reference supplied by KneadData
The database corresponds to T2T-CHM13v2.0
Approximate size: 3.6 GB

Example:
kneaddata_database --download human_genome bowtie2 /home/dani/kneaddata_test/database
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
3.4 4. Input requirements
This workflow assumes paired-end FASTQ input files.
Generic paired input:
sample_R1.fastq.gz
sample_R2.fastq.gz
Example real inputs:
/home/dani/kneaddata_test/input/36522947_R1.fastq.gz
/home/dani/kneaddata_test/input/36522947_R2.fastq.gz
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
3.5 5. Run KneadData on paired-end reads

3.5.1 5.1 Basic paired-end command
kneaddata \
  --input1 seq1.fastq.gz \
  --input2 seq2.fastq.gz \
  -db $DATABASE \
  --output kneaddata_output


3.5.2 Argument description



Argument
Description




--input1
Forward mate (R1)


--input2
Reverse mate (R2)


-db
Path to the KneadData/Bowtie2 contaminant database


--output
Directory where output files will be written
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
3.6 6. Real execution examples

3.6.1 Sample 1
kneaddata \
  --input1 /home/dani/kneaddata_test/input/36522947_R1.fastq.gz \
  --input2 /home/dani/kneaddata_test/input/36522947_R2.fastq.gz \
  -db /home/dani/kneaddata_test/database \
  --output /home/dani/kneaddata_test/output


3.6.2 Sample 2
kneaddata \
  --input1 /home/dani/kneaddata_test/input/36525128_R1.fastq.gz \
  --input2 /home/dani/kneaddata_test/input/36525128_R2.fastq.gz \
  -db /home/dani/kneaddata_test/database \
  --output /home/dani/kneaddata_test/output
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
3.7 7. Parallelization and performance tuning
KneadData allows control over computational resources.
kneaddata \
  --input1 seq1.fastq.gz \
  --input2 seq2.fastq.gz \
  -db $DATABASE \
  --output kneaddata_output \
  -t 16 \
  -p 8

3.7.1 Relevant options







Option
Meaning




-t
Number of threads used by underlying tools such as Bowtie2/Trimmomatic


-p
Number of parallel processes



These parameters should be adjusted according to available CPU and memory resources.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 3.8 8. Batch execution over all samples in an input directory
text:
3.8 8. Batch execution over all samples in an input directory
If all paired-end reads are stored in the same input directory with a consistent naming convention (*_R1.fastq.gz and *_R2.fastq.gz), processing can be automated.
INPUT_DIR=/home/dani/kneaddata_test/input
OUTPUT_DIR=/home/dani/kneaddata_test/output
DB_DIR=/home/dani/kneaddata_test/database

for r1 in ${INPUT_DIR}/*_R1.fastq.gz; do
  base=$(basename "${r1}" _R1.fastq.gz)

  kneaddata \
    --input1 "${INPUT_DIR}/${base}_R1.fastq.gz" \
    --input2 "${INPUT_DIR}/${base}_R2.fastq.gz" \
    -db "${DB_DIR}" \
    --output "${OUTPUT_DIR}"
done
This assumes each sample has exactly one forward file and one reverse file.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 3.9 9. Main output files produced by KneadData
text:
3.9 9. Main output files produced by KneadData
KneadData creates several intermediate and final files. The primary outputs of interest for downstream metagenomic profiling are the paired clean files.

3.9.1 9.1 Main paired outputs
Typical filenames:
36522947_R1_kneaddata_paired_1.fastq
36522947_R1_kneaddata_paired_2.fastq
These files contain reads that:

remain properly paired after trimming
passed quality control
did not align to the host reference database

These are the clean paired-end reads that should be used as input for Meteor2.


3.9.2 9.2 Unmatched outputs
Typical filenames:
36522947_R1_kneaddata_unmatched_1.fastq
36522947_R1_kneaddata_unmatched_2.fastq
These contain reads that survived filtering but lost their mate during trimming or decontamination. They may be useful in some contexts, but for the workflow documented here they are not used downstream in Meteor2.


3.9.3 9.3 Intermediate files
KneadData also generates multiple intermediate files, for example:

decompressed FASTQ files
reformatted identifier files
trimmed paired and trimmed single files
Bowtie2 clean intermediate files

These are useful for auditing and troubleshooting, but they are not the final files intended for profiling.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 3.10 10. Important naming caveat in KneadData outputs
text:
3.10 10. Important naming caveat in KneadData outputs
KneadData uses the basename of --input1 as the prefix for all generated outputs. This means that output filenames may look confusing at first sight.
Example:
36522947_R1_kneaddata_paired_2.fastq
Even though the file ends in _paired_2.fastq and corresponds to the reverse mate, its prefix still contains R1 because KneadData derives the output prefix from the first input file.
This does not indicate a technical problem, but the filenames are not ideal for downstream clarity.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
3.11 11. Recommended renaming strategy
To standardize the naming and make downstream processing easier, it is advisable to rename or symlink the paired clean files.

3.11.1 Example renaming
mv 36522947_R1_kneaddata_paired_1.fastq 36522947_clean_R1.fastq
mv 36522947_R1_kneaddata_paired_2.fastq 36522947_clean_R2.fastq
A consistent naming convention is strongly recommended before importing the files into Meteor2.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
3.12 12. Optional use of --output-prefix
KneadData also supports explicit output naming through an output prefix.
kneaddata \
  --input1 seq1.fastq.gz \
  --input2 seq2.fastq.gz \
  -db $DATABASE \
  --output kneaddata_output \
  --output-prefix sample_name
This can make the generated filenames more predictable and easier to standardize.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 3.13 13. Practical summary of the KneadData stage
text:
3.13 13. Practical summary of the KneadData stage
At the end of the KneadData stage, the files that should be retained for downstream profiling are the paired clean FASTQ files:
*_kneaddata_paired_1.fastq
*_kneaddata_paired_2.fastq
These represent:

high-quality reads
host-depleted reads
paired-end reads suitable for microbial profiling
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
4.1 14. Purpose
Meteor2 is a platform for quantitative shotgun metagenomic profiling of complex microbial ecosystems. It relies on ecosystem-specific microbial gene catalogues and supports:

taxonomic profiling
functional profiling
optional strain-level analysis

For the present workflow, Meteor2 is used after KneadData to profile human gut microbiome shotgun data.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
4.2 15. Installation
Meteor2 can be installed in a dedicated Conda environment.
conda create --name meteor -c conda-forge -c bioconda meteor -y
conda activate meteor
A separate environment from KneadData is recommended to avoid dependency conflicts.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
4.3 16. Catalogue download
Meteor2 requires a gene catalogue matching the ecosystem under study.
For human gut samples, the corresponding catalogue is:
hs_10_4_gut

4.3.1 16.1 Recommended catalogue download with Meteor2
Meteor provides an internal command to download, verify, and prepare catalogues.

4.3.1.1 Full catalogue
meteor download -i hs_10_4_gut -o /home/dani/meteor2_test/catalogues -c


4.3.1.2 Light / fast catalogue
meteor download -i hs_10_4_gut --fast -o /home/dani/meteor2_test/catalogues -c



4.3.2 Why this is the preferred method
Using meteor download is preferable because it:

downloads the correct catalogue version
verifies file integrity with checksum validation
prepares the catalogue automatically
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 4.4 17. Alternative manual catalogue download and extraction
text:
4.4 17. Alternative manual catalogue download and extraction
If the catalogue has already been downloaded manually, the corresponding files may look like this:
hs_10_4_gut.tar.xz
hs_10_4_gut_taxo.tar.xz
In that case, they must be extracted manually.
cd /home/dani/meteor2_test/catalogues

tar -xvf hs_10_4_gut.tar.xz
tar -xvf hs_10_4_gut_taxo.tar.xz
This step is only necessary if the catalogue was not obtained through meteor download.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
4.5 18. Input requirements for Meteor2
Meteor2 does not start directly from a single FASTQ pair passed to a generic top-level command. Instead, the workflow is divided into multiple subcommands.
The first step is FASTQ import/indexing with meteor fastq.
Meteor expects a directory containing input FASTQ files. For paired-end data, filenames must follow one of the accepted patterns:
sample_R1.fastq and sample_R2.fastq
or
sample_1.fastq and sample_2.fastq

4.5.1 Important implication for KneadData outputs
KneadData output names such as:
36522947_R1_kneaddata_paired_1.fastq
36522947_R1_kneaddata_paired_2.fastq
can still be compatible with Meteor2 because the suffix pattern _1.fastq / _2.fastq is present. However, for clarity, traceability, and standardization, it is strongly recommended to rename or symlink the KneadData outputs into a simpler, explicit paired-end convention before running meteor fastq.


4.5.2 Suggested Meteor input naming
36522947_R1.fastq
36522947_R2.fastq
36525128_R1.fastq
36525128_R2.fastq
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 4.6 19. Avoiding file duplication with symbolic links
text:
4.6 19. Avoiding file duplication with symbolic links
Since KneadData outputs may already consume substantial disk space, one practical strategy is to create symbolic links instead of copying files.

4.6.1 Example
mkdir -p /home/dani/meteor2_test/input
cd /home/dani/meteor2_test/input

ln -s /path/to/36522947_R1_kneaddata_paired_1.fastq 36522947_R1.fastq
ln -s /path/to/36522947_R1_kneaddata_paired_2.fastq 36522947_R2.fastq

ln -s /path/to/36525128_R1_kneaddata_paired_1.fastq 36525128_R1.fastq
ln -s /path/to/36525128_R1_kneaddata_paired_2.fastq 36525128_R2.fastq
This preserves storage and keeps the Meteor input directory clean and standardized.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 4.7 20. Step 1: import and index FASTQ files with meteor fastq
text:
4.7 20. Step 1: import and index FASTQ files with meteor fastq
This command prepares a Meteor-compatible FASTQ repository from the input directory.
meteor fastq \
  -i /home/dani/meteor2_test/input \
  -p \
  -o /home/dani/meteor2_test/results/fastq

4.7.1 Meaning of the arguments



Argument
Description




-i
Directory containing FASTQ files


-p
Indicates that the FASTQ files are paired-end


-o
Output directory for the Meteor FASTQ repository





4.7.2 Notes

Meteor2 expects the directory of FASTQ files, not an individual FASTQ file here
The output will contain one subdirectory per imported sample/library
If multiple sequencing runs belong to the same library, the -m option can be used to group them using a regular expression
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 4.8 21. Step 2: mapping against the gene catalogue with meteor mapping
text:
4.8 21. Step 2: mapping against the gene catalogue with meteor mapping
After FASTQ import, each sample must be mapped separately against the selected catalogue.

4.8.1 Example for one sample
meteor mapping \
  -i /home/dani/meteor2_test/results/fastq/36522947_R1_kneaddata_paired \
  -r /home/dani/meteor2_test/catalogues/hs_10_4_gut \
  -o /home/dani/meteor2_test/results/mapping \
  -t 6


4.8.2 Important note
The input of meteor mapping is not the original FASTQ file. It is the sample directory generated by meteor fastq for a given sample.


4.8.3 Meaning of the arguments







Argument
Description




-i
Directory corresponding to one imported sample in the Meteor FASTQ repository


-r
Catalogue/reference directory


-o
Output directory for mapping results


-t
Number of threads
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 4.9 22. Automating the mapping step across all samples
text:
4.9 22. Automating the mapping step across all samples
Because meteor mapping is run per sample directory, it should be automated when several samples are present.
FASTQ_REPO=/home/dani/meteor2_test/results/fastq
REF_DIR=/home/dani/meteor2_test/catalogues/hs_10_4_gut
MAP_DIR=/home/dani/meteor2_test/results/mapping

for sample_dir in ${FASTQ_REPO}/*; do
  if [ -d "${sample_dir}" ]; then
    meteor mapping \
      -i "${sample_dir}" \
      -r "${REF_DIR}" \
      -o "${MAP_DIR}" \
      -t 6
  fi
done
This loop processes all imported samples one by one.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 4.10 23. Step 3: taxonomic and functional profiling with meteor profile
text:
4.10 23. Step 3: taxonomic and functional profiling with meteor profile
Once mapping has been completed for a sample, taxonomic and functional profiling can be performed.

4.10.1 Example
meteor profile \
  -i /home/dani/meteor2_test/results/mapping \
  -r /home/dani/meteor2_test/catalogues/hs_10_4_gut \
  -o /home/dani/meteor2_test/results/profile \
  -n coverage


4.10.2 Meaning of the arguments







Argument
Description




-i
Directory containing mapped sample output


-r
Catalogue/reference directory


-o
Output directory for profiled sample results


-n coverage
Length-normalized abundance calculation using coverage normalization





4.10.3 Notes on normalization
The -n coverage option performs gene-length normalization and is the recommended mode in the standard Meteor workflow.
If omitted, no normalization is applied to the gene table.


4.10.4 Typical profile outputs
Depending on the catalogue type, this step can generate:

species abundance tables
ARD abundance tables
dbCAN abundance tables
gut metabolic module abundance tables
gut brain module abundance tables
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 4.11 24. Automating the profiling step across all mapped samples
text:
4.11 24. Automating the profiling step across all mapped samples
As with mapping, profiling is conceptually sample-based and should be applied to each mapped sample directory when needed.
MAP_DIR=/home/dani/meteor2_test/results/mapping
REF_DIR=/home/dani/meteor2_test/catalogues/hs_10_4_gut
PROFILE_DIR=/home/dani/meteor2_test/results/profile

for sample_dir in ${MAP_DIR}/*; do
  if [ -d "${sample_dir}" ]; then
    meteor profile \
      -i "${sample_dir}" \
      -r "${REF_DIR}" \
      -o "${PROFILE_DIR}" \
      -n coverage
  fi
done
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
section: 4.12 25. Step 4: merge all profiled samples with meteor merge
text:
4.12 25. Step 4: merge all profiled samples with meteor merge
Once all samples have been profiled, the outputs can be merged into multi-sample abundance tables.
meteor merge \
  -i /home/dani/meteor2_test/results/profile \
  -r /home/dani/meteor2_test/catalogues/hs_10_4_gut \
  -o /home/dani/meteor2_test/results/merge

4.12.1 Meaning of the arguments



Argument
Description




-i
Directory containing profiled sample subdirectories


-r
Catalogue/reference directory


-o
Output directory for merged tables





4.12.2 Output purpose
This step produces merged matrices suitable for downstream statistical analysis across samples.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
4.13 26. Optional downstream strain analysis
Meteor2 also supports strain-level analysis, although this is optional and outside the core taxonomic/functional workflow described above.

4.13.1 Strain inference
meteor strain \
  -i <mapping_sample_dir> \
  -o <strain_dir> \
  -r <refdir>


4.13.2 Strain tree computation
meteor tree \
  -i <strain_dir> \
  -o <tree_dir>
These steps are only relevant if strain-resolved comparisons are part of the study design.
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
5.1 Example layout
project/
├── kneaddata/
│   ├── input/
│   ├── database/
│   └── output/
├── meteor2/
│   ├── catalogues/
│   ├── input/
│   ├── output/
│   └── results/
│       ├── fastq/
│       ├── mapping/
│       ├── profile/
│       └── merge/
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
6.1 27. General recommendations

6.1.1 27.1 Keep the paired clean FASTQ files from KneadData
For this workflow, the correct files to propagate downstream are:
*_kneaddata_paired_1.fastq
*_kneaddata_paired_2.fastq
Do not use the unmatched files unless there is a specific analytical reason to process single-end survivors separately.


6.1.2 27.2 Standardize names before Meteor import
Even if Meteor can recognize _1 and _2, cleaner names simplify the workflow and reduce ambiguity.


6.1.3 27.3 Prefer symbolic links over duplication when possible
Large shotgun datasets quickly become storage-intensive. Symlinks allow a clean and reproducible organization without copying files.


6.1.4 27.4 Keep environments separate
Use independent Conda environments for KneadData and Meteor2.


6.1.5 27.5 Document the exact catalogue version used
Catalogue versioning is critical for reproducibility because Meteor2 relies on environment-specific references.


6.1.6 27.6 Record all parameters used
At minimum, document:

KneadData database version
KneadData thread/process settings
Meteor catalogue name and version
Meteor normalization mode
software versions and execution dates
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
7.1 28. Conceptual flow
Raw paired-end shotgun FASTQ
        ↓
KneadData
  - trimming
  - repetitive sequence filtering
  - human read removal
        ↓
Clean paired FASTQ
        ↓
Meteor fastq
        ↓
Meteor mapping
        ↓
Meteor profile
        ↓
Meteor merge
        ↓
Multi-sample taxonomic and functional abundance tables
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
8.1 29. KneadData
conda create -n kneaddata -c bioconda kneaddata -y
conda activate kneaddata
kneaddata_database --download human_genome bowtie2 /path/to/database

kneaddata \
  --input1 sample_R1.fastq.gz \
  --input2 sample_R2.fastq.gz \
  -db /path/to/database \
  --output /path/to/kneaddata_output
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2
text:
8.2 30. Meteor2
conda create --name meteor -c conda-forge -c bioconda meteor -y
conda activate meteor

meteor download -i hs_10_4_gut -o /path/to/catalogues -c

meteor fastq \
  -i /path/to/input_fastq_dir \
  -p \
  -o /path/to/results/fastq

meteor mapping \
  -i /path/to/results/fastq/sample_dir \
  -r /path/to/catalogues/hs_10_4_gut \
  -o /path/to/results/mapping \
  -t 6

meteor profile \
  -i /path/to/results/mapping/sample_dir \
  -r /path/to/catalogues/hs_10_4_gut \
  -o /path/to/results/profile \
  -n coverage

meteor merge \
  -i /path/to/results/profile \
  -r /path/to/catalogues/hs_10_4_gut \
  -o /path/to/results/merge
crumbs: 2  Shotgun metagenomics preprocessing and profiling workflow with KneadData and Meteor2

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.1 Background
A comprehensive understanding of microbial communities requires the integration of taxonomic, functional, and strain-level profiling (TFSP). In shotgun metagenomics, these three layers are tightly connected: taxonomy identifies who is there, functional profiling estimates what they can do, and strain-level analysis provides insight into within-species variation and population structure.
Most reference-based metagenomic profilers rely on one of three broad strategies:
These approaches are often highly effective for taxonomic assignment, but they usually provide only indirect or partial access to functional interpretation. In practice, this means that taxonomic and functional profiling are frequently performed with separate tools and databases, which can increase workflow complexity and create inconsistencies between the two outputs.
This limitation is particularly evident in approaches designed for broad applicability across many ecosystems. Because these methods often depend on conserved marker genes, they gain generalizability at the cost of reduced sensitivity to ecosystem-specific functional variation. As a result, functional characterization often requires additional annotation workflows based on pan-genome, orthology, or pathway resources.
An alternative strategy is to use ecosystem-specific microbial gene catalogues. These catalogue-based approaches rely on smaller but highly informative reference collections and enable a more coherent integration of taxonomic and functional profiling. This is the conceptual framework on which Meteor2 is based (ghozlane2025meteor2?).
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
whole-genome reference databases,
species-specific marker genes, or
universal single-copy marker genes.
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.2 What is Meteor2?
Meteor2 is a tool for quantitative metagenomic profiling of complex ecosystems based on microbial gene catalogues. Rather than relying primarily on universal marker genes, Meteor2 quantifies genes by mapping reads against ecosystem-specific reference catalogues and derives taxonomic, functional, and strain-level profiles from the same reference framework (ghozlane2025meteor2?).
Its main analytical unit is the Metagenomic Species Pan-genome (MSP), originally introduced with MSPminer (plazaonate2019mspminer?). In this framework, genes are grouped into microbial species-level units based on co-abundance patterns across samples. Within each MSP, a subset of highly connected genes is defined as signature genes, which are used as the most robust indicators for MSP detection and quantification.
Meteor2 uses microbial gene catalogues to perform:

species-level taxonomic profiling (Bacteria, Archaea, and Eukaryotes),
functional profiling,
strain-level population structure inference.

In addition, Meteor2 integrates several functional annotation repertoires into its catalogues, including:

KEGG Orthologs (KO) from KEGG database (kanehisa2016kegg?),
carbohydrante-active enzymes (CAZymes) from dbCAN (yuan2023dbcan?),
antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) from MUSTARD (roux2019mustard?).

These annotations can be quantified either at the individual gene level or at the MSP level, thereby linking taxonomic composition to functional potential within a unified framework.
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.3 Microbial gene catalogues
Meteor2 currently supports 10 curated microbial gene catalogues, each tailored to a specific host-associated ecosystem (ghozlane2025meteor2?). These catalogues cover:

human microbiomes: gut, oral, and skin,
domestic animals: cat and dog,
laboratory models: mouse and rat,
livestock: chicken, pig, and rabbit.

Each catalogue includes two main components:

MSPs, in which genes are clustered by co-abundance;
unclustered genes, which may represent biological content that is not captured by metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) or stable MSP structures (coelho2022biogeography?).

Meteor2 also incorporates three types of higher-level functional module systems:

Gut Brain Modules (GBMs) (vallescolomer2019neuroactive?),
Gut Metabolic Modules (GMMs) (vieirasilva2016species?),
KEGG modules (kanehisa2012keggmapper?).

For rapid analysis, Meteor2 additionally provides a light / fast mode, based on a reduced catalogue containing only 100 signature genes per MSP. These genes correspond to the most connected genes within each MSP and allow accelerated taxonomic and strain-level profiling. Functional profiling is not available in this reduced mode (ghozlane2025meteor2?).
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.4 Overview of the workflow
Meteor2 estimates the abundance of genes, MSPs, and functions within microbial communities, and also reconstructs sample-specific strain variation.
The analytical workflow consists of four main steps (ghozlane2025meteor2?):

Download an ecosystem-specific microbial gene catalogue
Align short metagenomic reads to the catalogue
Compute abundance profiles for genes, MSPs, and functions
Reconstruct sample-specific MSP genotypes and estimate mutation rates

The pipeline starts by aligning metagenomic reads against a reference microbial gene catalogue using Bowtie2 (langmead2012bowtie2?). From the mapping results, Meteor2 derives gene abundance estimates and propagates these measurements to species- and function-level summaries. In parallel, a strain branch performs SNV calling and reconstructs sample-specific consensus sequences for downstream phylogenetic and distance-based analyses.



Overview of the Meteor2 pipeline for TFSP analysis. The workflow begins with read alignment against a microbial gene catalogue. One branch derives gene, MSP, and functional abundance profiles, while the second branch performs SNV calling and reconstructs sample-specific MSP sequences for downstream phylogenetic and mutation-rate analyses.


Figure 1. Overview of the Meteor2 pipeline for TFSP analysis.
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.5 Quantification modes
After read alignment, Meteor2 re-estimates gene counts using one of three counting modes:

unique: counts only reads with a single valid alignment.
total: assigns all alignments to all matched genes and sums them directly.
shared: redistributes multi-mapping reads proportionally across the genes they align to, based on the genes’ unique read counts. If all unique counts are zero, the read is distributed evenly across all compatible genes.

The shared mode is particularly useful when reads map to homologous genes and a proportional assignment strategy is preferred over strict unique counting.
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.6 Taxonomic profiling
Meteor2 derives taxonomic profiles from gene count tables through two main operations:

normalization,
reduction from genes to MSPs.


3.6.1 Normalization
Meteor2 supports the following normalization strategies:

Depth coverage: read count divided by gene length and multiplied by 100, with gene length corrected for edge effects due to read length (metahit2014catalog?).
FPKM (Fragments Per Kilobase Million).



3.6.2 Reduction to MSP abundance
MSP abundance is estimated by averaging the abundance of signature genes after normalization. By default, Meteor2 considers the 100 most central signature genes per MSP.
An MSP is assigned an abundance of 0 if too few signature genes are detected in the sample. In standard catalogues, the default requirement is more stringent; in light catalogues, the detection threshold is reduced, with a default requirement of 20% of signature genes.
This approach provides a robust species-level abundance estimate while minimizing noise from poorly supported genes.
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.7 Functional profiling
Meteor2 computes the abundance of functional entities in two complementary ways:

by aggregating the abundance of genes annotated with a given function,
or by aggregating the abundance of MSPs carrying that function.

When MSP-based functional inference is used, only genes that are actually detected in the sample (at least one mapped read) are considered.

3.7.1 Functional modules
Meteor2 supports module-level profiling for systems such as GBMs, GMMs, and KEGG modules. For a given module, an MSP is considered a module carrier when the module completeness exceeds 90% in that sample (ghozlane2025meteor2?).
Because some module steps can be satisfied by alternative functions, Meteor2 evaluates all compatible alternative configurations and retains the maximum completeness value. The total abundance of all MSP carriers defines the module potential in that sample, together with the list of contributing taxa.
This strategy provides an explicit bridge between community composition and functional potential, making it possible to identify not only which functions are present, but also which species are likely contributing to them.
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.8 Strain-level analysis
Meteor2 performs strain-level analysis in three main stages:

SNV calling using FreeBayes (garrison2012freebayes?)
Reconstruction of sample-specific MSP sequences
Pairwise comparison and phylogenetic analysis of reconstructed MSPs


3.8.1 1. SNV calling
Meteor2 identifies single nucleotide variants from mapped reads and uses them to build a sample-specific consensus gene catalogue.


3.8.2 2. Reconstruction of sample-specific MSPs
Only MSPs with sufficient coverage are retained for downstream strain analysis. By default, at least 80 out of 100 signature genes must have a minimum breadth coverage of 0.5.


3.8.3 3. Mutation rate and distance estimation
MSPs detected in at least two samples are considered for mutation-rate analysis. By default, at least 10 informative sites are required to estimate distances. Pairwise distances between sample-specific MSP sequences are then computed under a General Time Reversible (GTR) evolutionary model, allowing strain similarity and divergence to be quantified (ghozlane2025meteor2?).
Meteor2 can also generate phylogenetic trees from these reconstructed sequences.
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.9 Installation
Meteor2 can be installed with conda, including its dependencies:
conda create --name meteor -c conda-forge -c bioconda meteor
Alternatively, it can be installed with pip using a recent Python version:
pip install meteor
To test the installation:
meteor test
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.10 Nextflow wrapper
Meteor2 also provides a Nextflow wrapper (nf-meteor.nf) for automated execution of the full workflow (meteorgithub?).
nf-meteor.nf --in <fastq_dir> --catalogue_name <catalogue_name> --out <output_dir> --cpus <nb_cpus> -w <temp_work_dir>

3.10.1 Main parameters

--in: directory containing paired fastq.gz files
--out: output directory
--cpus: number of CPUs to use
--catalogue_name: name of a prebuilt catalogue
--catalogue: path to a custom catalogue (overrides --catalogue_name)
--fast: enable fast mode (no functional analysis)
--check_catalogue: check whether the catalogue is compatible with the input reads

Allowed --catalogue_name values include:

fc_1_3_gut
gg_13_6_caecal
clf_1_0_gut
hs_10_4_gut
hs_8_4_oral
hs_2_9_skin
mm_5_0_gut
oc_5_7_gut
rn_5_9_gut
ssc_9_3_gut
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.11 Getting started
A basic Meteor2 workflow typically involves the following steps:

Download or build a reference catalogue
Import and index FASTQ files
Map reads against the reference catalogue
Compute taxonomic and functional abundance profiles
Perform strain-level profiling
Merge outputs across samples when needed
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.12 1. Download a reference catalogue
Meteor2 requires a locally available microbial gene catalogue, either in full or light format.

The full catalogue contains all genes and supports taxonomic, functional, and strain-level profiling.
The light catalogue contains only marker/signature genes and supports rapid taxonomic and strain-level profiling, but not functional profiling.


3.12.1 Available catalogues

Download a full catalogue with:
meteor download -i <name> -c -o <refdir>
Download a light catalogue with:
meteor download -i <name> -c --fast -o <refdir>
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.13 2. Import FASTQ files
Meteor2 requires an initial FASTQ indexing step:
meteor fastq -i <fastqdir> [-p paired reads] -o <outputdir>
When multiple sequencing files are available for the same library, the -m option can be used to group them automatically.
For example, if the directory contains:
Illumina_lib1-SAMPLE_01.fastq
Illumina_lib1-SAMPLE_02.fastq
Illumina_lib2-SAMPLE_01.fastq
Illumina_lib2-SAMPLE_02.fastq
The following command will group files belonging to the same sample/library structure:
meteor fastq -i ./ -m SAMPLE_\\d+ -o outputdir
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.14 3. Mapping
FASTQ files are mapped against a reference catalogue to generate gene count tables:
meteor mapping -i <fastqdir/sampledir> -r <refdir> -o <mappingdir>
Before this step, it is recommended to remove:

low-quality reads,
short reads (e.g. < 60 nt),
host-derived reads.
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.15 4. Taxonomic and functional profiling
Genes in the catalogue are clustered into Metagenomic Species Pan-genomes (MSPs) with MSPminer (plazaonate2019mspminer?) and functionally annotated against:

KEGG (kanehisa2016kegg?),
dbCAN (yuan2023dbcan?),
MUSTARD (roux2019mustard?).

MSP and functional profiles are computed from the gene count table with:
meteor profile -i <mappingdir/sampledir> -o <profiledir> -r <refdir> -n coverage
The -n parameter enables read-count normalization by gene length. If omitted, no normalization is applied to the gene table.
This profiling step can generate:

a species abundance table,
an ARG abundance table (full catalogue only),
a dbCAN abundance table (full catalogue only),
a Gut Metabolic Modules (GMM) abundance table (full catalogue only),
a Gut Brain Modules (GBM) abundance table (full catalogue only).
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
text:
3.17 6. Strain profiling
Meteor2 can profile strains across large metagenomic datasets by identifying sample-specific mutations and projecting them onto MSP reference sequences.
To perform strain profiling:
meteor strain -i <mappingdir/sampledir> -o <straindir> -r <refdir>
To compute mutation rates and build trees between strains:
meteor tree -i <straindir> -o <treedir>
This step generates:

a mutation rate matrix,
a FASTA file for each reconstructed strain in each sample,
a detailed pairwise comparison table for each strain.


3.17.1 Pairwise strain comparison table







Column
Description




sample1
First sample in the comparison


sample2
Second sample in the comparison


total_length
Total length of the alignment (in bases)


overlap_noN_info_count
Number of positions where both samples have minimal information (A, C, G, T or IUPAC codes excluding N, gaps, and ?)


overlap_noIUPAC_info_count
Number of positions where both samples have maximal information (strictly A, C, G, T)


overlap_noN_info_pc
Percentage of total_length with minimal-information overlap


overlap_noIUPAC_info_pc
Percentage of total_length with maximal-information overlap


noN_info_pc_sample1
In sample1, percentage of positions with minimal information


noN_info_pc_sample2
In sample2, percentage of positions with minimal information


noIUPAC_info_pc_sample1
In sample1, percentage of positions with maximal information


noIUPAC_info_pc_sample2
In sample2, percentage of positions with maximal information


distance
Genetic distance between samples (0.0 = identical)


distance_category
Categorical classification: same_strain, same_subspecies, or divergent





3.17.2 Distance-based classification
Samples are automatically classified according to their genetic distance:









Category
Distance threshold
Approximate similarity
Biological interpretation




same_strain
≤ 0.0001
≥ 99.99%
Same strain / clone


same_subspecies
≤ 0.015
≥ 97%
Same subspecies


divergent
> 0.015
< 97%
Different lineage



Notes

Maximal information: unambiguous nucleotides only (A, C, G, T)
Minimal information: includes IUPAC ambiguity codes (R, Y, S, W, K, M, B, D, H, V) but excludes N, gaps (-), and unknown characters (?)
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 3  Meteor2 documentation
crumbs: 3  Meteor2 documentation

title: 5  RNA Integrity Number (RIN): technical background
text:
The complete quality control results for all submitted samples are summarized in Table 5.1, including RNA concentration, total RNA amount, RNA Integrity Number (RIN), and final QC status. Representative electropherograms generated during the analysis are shown in Figure 5.1.






Note









Figure 5.1: Representative RNA electropherograms illustrating different levels of RNA integrity (RIN = 10, 5, and 3).



The RNA Integrity Number (RIN) is a numerical score ranging from 1 (fully degraded RNA) to 10 (intact RNA), widely used to assess total RNA quality. RIN values are computed from automated electrophoretic analyses performed on platforms such as the Agilent Bioanalyzer.
The RIN algorithm evaluates multiple features of the electropherogram, including:

The presence, height, and sharpness of ribosomal RNA peaks (primarily 18S and 28S rRNA in eukaryotes). High-quality RNA typically displays well-defined 18S and 28S peaks with an approximate 2:1 intensity ratio.
Background signal and degree of fragmentation. Degraded RNA is characterized by reduced or absent ribosomal peaks and increased signal corresponding to low-molecular-weight fragments.
Overall signal distribution across fragment sizes, reflecting progressive RNA degradation.

In high-quality samples, electropherograms show sharp and well-resolved rRNA peaks with low background noise. In contrast, degraded samples exhibit flattened or missing rRNA peaks and a pronounced accumulation of short RNA fragments.


Most samples exhibited RIN values between 7.0 and 9.8, which are generally considered suitable for RNA-Seq library construction and downstream differential gene expression analyses. RNA concentrations also fell within acceptable ranges for library preparation, although moderate variability among samples was observed.
Two samples deviated from the expected quality standards:

IT12 showed a low RIN value (3.8), indicating substantial RNA degradation. This sample was classified as Fail and deemed unsuitable for RNA-Seq.
IT16 displayed an intermediate RIN value of 6.0, suggesting partial degradation that could compromise sequencing quality and data interpretation.

Additionally, ITPNK exhibited slight genomic DNA contamination, as indicated in the Novogene QC report. Despite this observation, the sample passed overall QC criteria.
Given that the sequencing budget covered 24 RNA-Seq libraries and 26 samples were initially submitted, samples IT12 and IT16 were excluded from sequencing. The remaining samples met the quality requirements for transcriptomic analysis and were retained for RNA-Seq library preparation and sequencing.

Figure: RNA quality control electropherograms generated using the Agilent 5400 Bioanalyzer. Each tab corresponds to one sample and displays the characteristic 18S and 28S rRNA peaks and fragment size distribution used to assess RNA integrity prior to sequencing.










NoteTechnical notes from the Novogene RNA QC report



Q1. What is the influence of RNA concentration on library construction?
RNA concentration determines the total RNA input available for library preparation. Insufficient RNA input may result in low library yield or library construction failure. Additional RNA concentration steps may increase degradation risk.
Q2. Does genomic DNA contamination affect poly(A)-enriched mRNA library preparation?
Genomic DNA is not retained during poly(A) enrichment–based mRNA library preparation. Therefore, low-level genomic DNA contamination does not affect downstream library construction when samples pass QC.
Q3. What RNA purification methods are used and how much RNA loss is expected?
RNA purification is typically performed using column-based or magnetic bead–based methods. RNA loss after purification is expected to be approximately 60%, depending on the purification strategy and initial RNA quality.
crumbs: 5  RNA Integrity Number (RIN): technical background
